refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation by the tele-operator.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation capability, and (2) ensuring latency is compatible with safety. The refined version separates the functional intent (enable teleoperation communication) from the performance constraint (latency compatible with safety). The phrase 'compatible with safe remote operation' remains in the FR because it defines the functional boundary—the system must deliver latency sufficient for the tele-operator to safely command and perceive feedback. The specific numerical threshold (p95 ≤ 150 ms) belongs in acceptance criteria, not the FR itself, as it is a quantified design constraint derived from safety analysis, not a functional requirement. The FR is now solution-neutral: it does not prescribe network architecture, protocol, or technology.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (sensor input → tele-operator perception + tele-operator command → equipment actuation) under agreed network conditions. Confirm that measured latency distribution (p95) meets the acceptance criterion of ≤150 ms. Validate through tele-operator trials that latency permits safe command execution and hazard response without inducing unsafe delays or operator error.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit tele-operator commands to the remote equipment with measurable end-to-end latency.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit equipment state and sensor feedback to the tele-operator with measurable end-to-end latency.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication availability under the agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Establish a dedicated, low-latency communication pathway with end-to-end latency monitoring and adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization to ensure operator commands and telemetry reach within the p95 ≤ 150 ms budget.

DP2: Implement a hierarchical control architecture where local autonomous agents execute operator commands with bounded execution time, reducing dependency on round-trip latency for safety-critical actions.

DP3: Deploy a hybrid communication strategy combining primary low-latency channel (for real-time control) and secondary resilient channel (for state synchronization and fallback), with automatic failover logic.
DP4: Use predictive state estimation and command buffering at the remote site to mask network jitter and transient latency spikes, maintaining operator perception of responsiveness.
DP5: Establish a time-synchronized, deterministic communication protocol with reserved bandwidth and priority queuing to guarantee latency bounds independent of background network traffic.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Deploy a hybrid communication strategy combining primary low-latency channel (for real-time control) and secondary resilient channel (for state synchronization and fallback), with automatic failover logic, integrated with DP5 time-synchronized deterministic protocol and DP4 predictive state estimation to achieve p95 ≤ 150 ms latency under agreed network conditions.

potential_sources: DP3 + DP5 coupling: Both address latency guarantees; mitigation: DP5 provides the protocol foundation, DP3 provides the architectural redundancy strategy—complementary, not redundant.
DP3 + DP4 coupling: State estimation depends on channel reliability; mitigation: DP4 operates independently on received telemetry; DP3 ensures delivery; loose coupling acceptable.
DP3 + worksite safety plan: Failover logic must align with operational policies; mitigation: failover behavior (e.g., safe stop vs. hold) must be defined in safety plan, not embedded in DP.
DP5 + network conditions assumption: Deterministic protocol assumes reserved bandwidth; if not available, DP3 fallback to secondary channel mitigates risk.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 is selected because it decouples latency guarantee from single-point-of-failure (primary channel) and allows graceful degradation. DP5 ensures the primary channel meets the p95 bound; DP4 ensures operator experience remains acceptable during transients. No circular dependencies detected. Architectural independence maintained through clear channel responsibilities.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: 'Agreed network conditions' are stable and measurable (e.g., bandwidth reservation, maximum jitter, packet loss rate). RISK: If conditions are undefined or variable, p95 ≤ 150 ms cannot be validated. MITIGATION: Require explicit network SLA or characterization before design finalization.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits a failover mode (e.g., safe stop, hold position, or operator manual override). RISK: If no safe failover exists, DP3 secondary channel is ineffective. MITIGATION: Coordinate with safety plan early; define failover behavior as a functional requirement.

ASSUMPTION: Operator cognitive load and reaction time are compatible with 150 ms latency. RISK: If operator requires <100 ms for safety-critical tasks, requirement is unachievable. MITIGATION: Validate p95 ≤ 150 ms against operator studies or domain standards (e.g., ISO/IEC 23601 for teleoperation).

ASSUMPTION: Predictive state estimation (DP4) can be trained or tuned for the specific worksite environment. RISK: If environment is highly dynamic or unpredictable, estimation error may degrade operator trust. MITIGATION: Include estimation confidence metrics in operator feedback; allow operator to disable prediction if needed.

ASSUMPTION: Time synchronization across primary and secondary channels is achievable (DP5). RISK: If synchronization drift exceeds protocol tolerance, latency guarantees fail. MITIGATION: Specify synchronization accuracy requirement (e.g., ±10 ms) and include clock discipline mechanism.

ASSUMPTION: Bandwidth and priority queuing can be reserved on 'agreed network' without interference from other traffic. RISK: If network is shared or unmanaged, QoS guarantees are unenforceable. MITIGATION: Require network isolation or traffic shaping at deployment.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions (specify: bandwidth, jitter, packet loss, synchronization tolerance), supporting safe remote operation by the tele-operator as defined in the worksite safety plan. The solution shall include a primary deterministic low-latency control channel and a secondary resilient state-synchronization channel with automatic failover to a safe operational mode (defined in safety plan). Operator feedback shall include state estimation confidence metrics to maintain situational awareness during network transients.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates hybrid communication strategy with dual-channel architecture (primary low-latency + secondary resilient), automatic failover logic, time-synchronized deterministic protocol integration, and predictive state estimation for real-time teleoperated control with p95 ≤ 150 ms latency guarantee.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication with primary (low-latency) and secondary (resilient) channels; carries control commands outbound and telemetry/state inbound; failover logic selects active channel based on latency and availability metrics.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives vehicle state, sensor data, and network condition metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss) to feed predictive state estimation (DP4) and time-synchronization protocol (DP5).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Transmits deterministic control commands to Vehicle based on hybrid channel selection and failover decisions; ensures real-time control semantics.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and alerts; may transmit safety-critical state via secondary resilient channel if primary fails; coordinates with Safety Services on communication health.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Teleoperation is the natural owner of dual-channel communication strategy, failover logic, and latency-critical control. DP3 integrates DP4 (predictive state estimation) and DP5 (time-synchronized protocol) as internal mechanisms. Primary diffusion: SafetyAlert interface (safety constraints must be coordinated with Safety Services), and Network Link abstraction (physical channel management may require Vehicle-level coordination). Minimal diffusion overall.

Poor fit: Vehicle is the target of control commands, not the owner of teleoperation communication strategy. Placing DP3 here would scatter communication logic across vehicle-side and operator-side, violating cohesion. High diffusion: failover logic, latency monitoring, and predictive estimation would need to diffuse to Teleoperation module anyway. Creates bidirectional dependency and unclear ownership.

Poor fit: Safety Services owns safety constraints and alerts, not communication strategy. DP3 is fundamentally about communication resilience and latency, not safety policy enforcement. High diffusion: hybrid channel selection, failover logic, and time-synchronization would need to diffuse to Teleoperation. Creates tight coupling between safety and communication layers, reducing modularity.

Very poor fit: NGHE Solution is a system-level container, not a functional module. Placing DP3 here provides no architectural clarity and forces all communication logic to diffuse into lower-level modules (Teleoperation, Vehicle, Safety Services). Violates principle of most-specific boundary. Creates a catch-all anti-pattern.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of the hybrid communication strategy because: (1) it is responsible for real-time control command transmission and state reception under latency constraints; (2) failover logic and channel selection are operator-side concerns, not vehicle-side; (3) predictive state estimation (DP4) and time-synchronized protocol (DP5) are internal mechanisms that support teleoperation fidelity; (4) the p95 ≤ 150 ms latency requirement is a teleoperation-specific SLA, not a vehicle or safety concern. Containment is strong (0.92) with minimal diffusion (0.08) limited to safety coordination and network abstraction.
containment_risks: SafetyAlert interface coupling: Safety Services must validate that failover decisions do not violate safety constraints. Requires clear contract on safety-critical state transmission via secondary channel. Mitigate with explicit safety-aware failover policy in Teleoperation.

Network Link abstraction: Physical dual-channel provisioning (primary + secondary) may require Vehicle-level coordination (e.g., dual modems, network interface selection). Mitigate by defining Network Link as a clean abstraction that hides physical channel management from Teleoperation logic.

Time-synchronization (DP5) diffusion: If DP5 requires Vehicle-side clock synchronization or network-level timestamping, some DP5 logic may diffuse to Vehicle or network infrastructure. Mitigate by centralizing synchronization protocol in Teleoperation and using Network Link interface to propagate timestamps.

Predictive state estimation (DP4) data dependency: DP4 requires high-fidelity telemetry input. If Telemetry interface is lossy or delayed, predictive accuracy degrades, undermining failover decisions. Mitigate with explicit telemetry quality SLA and fallback estimation strategies.

Latency measurement and monitoring: Achieving p95 ≤ 150 ms requires continuous latency telemetry from both channels. If latency metrics are not reliably available via Telemetry interface, failover logic becomes reactive rather than predictive. Mitigate with explicit latency telemetry contract.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP3 is well-formulated and containment is strong in Teleoperation. No revision needed. However, to reduce SafetyAlert coupling risk, consider decomposing DP3 into two sub-DPs: (1) DP3a: Hybrid communication channel selection and failover logic (owned by Teleoperation); (2) DP3b: Safety-aware failover policy enforcement (owned by Safety Services, invoked by Teleoperation via SafetyAlert). This preserves Teleoperation ownership of communication strategy while making safety constraints explicit and testable. Alternatively, if Vehicle-side network management is complex, define a Network Management sub-module within Vehicle to own physical dual-channel provisioning, with Teleoperation owning logical failover strategy. This maintains clean separation of concerns.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms latency under agreed network conditions). The IM boundary (Teleoperation module) is the natural owner of this SLA. Verification is feasible via instrumented latency telemetry on both primary and secondary channels, with statistical aggregation to p95 percentile. The Telemetry interface explicitly carries latency and jitter metrics, enabling continuous monitoring. Traceability from FR → DP3 → Teleoperation IM is clear and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The Teleoperation module is the most-specific, cohesive owner of the hybrid communication strategy. Containment score of 0.92 reflects strong fit. The FR requirement (safe remote operation via teleoperation) maps directly to the DP (hybrid dual-channel strategy with failover) and IM (Teleoperation module boundary). Ownership is unambiguous. External interfaces (Network Link, Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and directional, enabling clear responsibility boundaries.

diffusion_risks: SafetyAlert coupling: Failover decisions in Teleoperation may conflict with safety constraints enforced by Safety Services. If a failover to secondary channel violates a safety policy (e.g., reduced bandwidth for safety-critical state), the decision logic becomes bidirectionally dependent. Mitigation: Define explicit safety-aware failover policy as a contract between Teleoperation and Safety Services; Safety Services pre-approves failover scenarios during initialization.

Network Link abstraction leakage: Physical dual-channel provisioning (primary low-latency + secondary resilient) may require Vehicle-side network interface management (e.g., dual modems, link selection logic). If this logic diffuses to Vehicle module, DP3 ownership becomes split. Mitigation: Encapsulate physical channel management within Network Link abstraction; Teleoperation interacts only with logical channel semantics, not hardware details.

Time-synchronized deterministic protocol (DP5) diffusion: DP5 integration requires clock synchronization across operator and vehicle. If Vehicle-side clock discipline or network-level timestamping is required, DP5 logic diffuses beyond Teleoperation. Mitigation: Centralize synchronization protocol logic in Teleoperation; use Network Link to propagate synchronized timestamps; Vehicle receives timestamped commands without owning synchronization logic.

Predictive state estimation (DP4) data dependency: DP4 requires high-fidelity, low-latency telemetry input. If Telemetry interface is lossy, delayed, or unreliable, predictive accuracy degrades, undermining failover decisions. This creates a hidden dependency on Telemetry quality that is not explicitly owned. Mitigation: Define explicit telemetry quality SLA (latency, completeness, freshness) as part of Telemetry interface contract; establish fallback estimation strategies if SLA is violated.

Latency measurement and monitoring diffusion: Achieving p95 ≤ 150 ms requires continuous, accurate latency telemetry from both channels. If latency metrics are not reliably available or are computed at Vehicle level, failover logic becomes reactive rather than predictive. Mitigation: Explicitly own latency telemetry collection and aggregation within Teleoperation; define Telemetry interface to guarantee latency metric availability and freshness.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module demonstrates strong containment (0.92) with minimal diffusion (0.08). DP3 is well-scoped: hybrid channel selection, failover logic, and latency-critical control are operator-side concerns that naturally belong in Teleoperation. Integration of DP4 (predictive state estimation) and DP5 (time-synchronized protocol) as internal mechanisms is appropriate; these are implementation details that support teleoperation fidelity, not separate functional concerns. The four external interfaces (Network Link, Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) are clearly defined and directional, enabling clean separation from Vehicle, Safety Services, and network infrastructure. Primary containment risk is SafetyAlert coupling, which is manageable via explicit safety-aware failover policy. Secondary risks (Network Link abstraction, DP5 diffusion, DP4 data dependency, latency monitoring) are all mitigable through clear interface contracts and ownership boundaries. No fundamental architectural flaw detected.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR requirement for safe remote teleoperation with latency-bounded communication. The DP specifies a concrete solution: hybrid dual-channel strategy (primary low-latency + secondary resilient) with automatic failover, integrated with time-synchronized protocol (DP5) and predictive state estimation (DP4), targeting p95 ≤ 150 ms latency. The DP is sufficiently detailed to be implementable and verifiable. The acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions) is explicitly referenced in the DP, enabling traceability. The DP does not over-constrain the solution (e.g., does not mandate specific protocols or hardware) while providing sufficient guidance for architectural decisions. Coupling to DP4 and DP5 is explicit and justified: DP4 enables predictive failover decisions; DP5 ensures deterministic control semantics. No logical gaps or contradictions detected between FR and DP.

recommended_revisions: Decompose DP3 into two sub-DPs to reduce SafetyAlert coupling: (1) DP3a: Hybrid communication channel selection and automatic failover logic based on latency and availability metrics (owned by Teleoperation); (2) DP3b: Safety-aware failover policy enforcement that validates failover decisions against safety constraints (owned by Safety Services, invoked by Teleoperation via SafetyAlert interface). This preserves Teleoperation ownership of communication strategy while making safety constraints explicit, testable, and independently verifiable.

Formalize Telemetry interface contract to guarantee latency metric availability and freshness. Specify: (1) latency measurement granularity (per-packet, per-command, per-batch); (2) metric freshness SLA (e.g., latency telemetry delivered within 50 ms of measurement); (3) completeness guarantee (e.g., ≥99% of latency samples delivered); (4) fallback behavior if SLA is violated (e.g., conservative failover to secondary channel). This mitigates DP4 data dependency risk and ensures failover logic has reliable input.

Define Network Link abstraction to hide physical dual-channel provisioning from Teleoperation logic. Specify: (1) logical channel semantics (primary vs. secondary, with automatic selection based on failover policy); (2) physical channel management responsibility (owned by Vehicle or network infrastructure, not Teleoperation); (3) channel health metrics exposed to Teleoperation (latency, jitter, packet loss, availability); (4) failover latency SLA (e.g., channel switch completes within 50 ms). This mitigates Network Link abstraction leakage and maintains clean separation of concerns.

Explicitly document DP5 (time-synchronized deterministic protocol) integration points with Teleoperation. Specify: (1) synchronization protocol ownership (centralized in Teleoperation or distributed across Teleoperation + Vehicle); (2) timestamp propagation mechanism (via Network Link or Telemetry interface); (3) clock discipline requirements (e.g., Vehicle clock drift tolerance); (4) fallback behavior if synchronization fails (e.g., degraded control fidelity). This mitigates DP5 diffusion risk and clarifies ownership boundaries.

Add explicit latency monitoring and telemetry collection as a sub-responsibility within Teleoperation IM. Specify: (1) latency measurement points (e.g., command transmission, command reception at Vehicle, telemetry transmission, telemetry reception at operator); (2) aggregation strategy (e.g., sliding window for p95 computation); (3) alert thresholds (e.g., if p95 exceeds 150 ms for >30 seconds, trigger failover or safety alert); (4) monitoring interface (e.g., expose latency metrics to Safety Services and operator UI). This ensures latency SLA is actively monitored and violations are detected early.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, DP-IM, FR-DP) pass with high confidence, indicating the current FR and DP are logically sound and architecturally feasible. However, the recommended_revisions identify five critical refinements that must be applied before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. The revisions address: (1) SafetyAlert coupling via explicit safety-aware failover policy decomposition; (2) Telemetry interface contract formalization to eliminate DP4 data dependency risk; (3) Network Link abstraction clarification to prevent physical channel management diffusion; (4) DP5 integration point documentation to reduce synchronization logic diffusion; (5) explicit latency monitoring ownership within Teleoperation IM. Given high safety criticality, these revisions are non-optional: they establish the contractual and ownership boundaries necessary for safe, verifiable failover behavior. Proceeding to sub-FR decomposition without these revisions would propagate ambiguity into lower levels and create verification gaps at integration. The revisions are refinements to the current level, not fundamental rework; they clarify existing DP3 logic and interface contracts without changing the core FR or DP strategy.

revise.why: DP3 must be decomposed into two explicit sub-DPs to separate communication strategy (DP3a: Teleoperation-owned hybrid channel selection and failover) from safety-aware failover policy enforcement (DP3b: Safety Services-owned validation). This decomposition is justified by the diffusion risk analysis: SafetyAlert coupling indicates that failover decisions cannot be made unilaterally by Teleoperation without safety constraint validation. Formalizing this as a two-part DP with explicit interface contract (DP3a invokes DP3b via SafetyAlert) makes safety ownership explicit, testable, and prevents bidirectional dependency. Additionally, four interface contracts must be formalized: (1) Telemetry interface SLA (latency metric availability, freshness, completeness, fallback); (2) Network Link abstraction (logical channel semantics, physical management responsibility, health metrics, failover latency SLA); (3) DP5 integration specification (synchronization ownership, timestamp propagation, clock discipline, fallback); (4) latency monitoring sub-responsibility within Teleoperation IM (measurement points, aggregation, alert thresholds, monitoring interface). These revisions are essential to eliminate diffusion risks and establish verifiable ownership boundaries before sub-FR decomposition.

assumptions: SafetyAlert interface is bidirectional and supports synchronous validation of failover decisions by Safety Services before channel switch occurs.
Telemetry interface can reliably deliver latency metrics with <50 ms freshness and ≥99% completeness under nominal network conditions.
Network Link abstraction can hide physical dual-channel provisioning and expose only logical channel semantics (primary/secondary selection) to Teleoperation.
DP5 (time-synchronized deterministic protocol) synchronization logic can be centralized in Teleoperation without requiring Vehicle-side clock discipline ownership.
Latency monitoring and p95 aggregation can be performed continuously within Teleoperation IM without imposing unacceptable computational overhead.
Failover latency (channel switch completion) can be achieved within 50 ms, enabling p95 ≤ 150 ms SLA compliance even with failover events.
Safety Services can pre-approve failover scenarios during initialization, enabling deterministic safety-aware failover policy enforcement.
open_risks: SafetyAlert coupling: If Safety Services validation of failover decisions introduces latency >50 ms, failover responsiveness may degrade, violating p95 ≤ 150 ms SLA. Mitigation: Define synchronous validation SLA for SafetyAlert interface; pre-compute approved failover scenarios to enable fast validation.

Telemetry data dependency: If Telemetry interface cannot guarantee latency metric freshness <50 ms, predictive failover decisions (DP4) become reactive, reducing failover effectiveness. Mitigation: Formalize Telemetry SLA with explicit freshness guarantee; establish fallback estimation if SLA violated.

Network Link abstraction leakage: If Vehicle-side network interface management (dual modems, link selection) is required, DP3 ownership becomes split between Teleoperation and Vehicle. Mitigation: Encapsulate all physical channel management within Network Link abstraction; Teleoperation interacts only with logical channel semantics.

DP5 synchronization diffusion: If Vehicle-side clock discipline or network-level timestamping is required for DP5 integration, synchronization logic diffuses beyond Teleoperation. Mitigation: Centralize synchronization protocol logic in Teleoperation; use Network Link to propagate synchronized timestamps.

Latency monitoring overhead: If continuous p95 aggregation imposes unacceptable computational load on Teleoperation IM, monitoring may be deferred or sampled, reducing SLA verification confidence. Mitigation: Implement efficient sliding-window p95 algorithm; validate computational overhead during detailed design.

Failover latency budget: If channel switch completion requires >50 ms, p95 ≤ 150 ms SLA becomes difficult to achieve under failover scenarios. Mitigation: Establish failover latency SLA as part of Network Link contract; validate via network simulation and hardware testing.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of DP3a (hybrid channel selection and failover logic) and DP3b (safety-aware failover policy enforcement) with explicit interface contract via SafetyAlert.
Telemetry interface SLA document specifying latency metric availability, freshness (<50 ms), completeness (≥99%), and fallback behavior.
Network Link abstraction specification defining logical channel semantics, physical management responsibility, health metrics, and failover latency SLA (<50 ms).
DP5 integration specification documenting synchronization protocol ownership, timestamp propagation mechanism, clock discipline requirements, and fallback behavior.
Latency monitoring design specification within Teleoperation IM: measurement points, aggregation algorithm (sliding-window p95), alert thresholds, and monitoring interface.
Safety-aware failover policy document: pre-approved failover scenarios, validation rules, and synchronous validation SLA for SafetyAlert interface.
Network simulation or testbed results validating p95 ≤ 150 ms SLA compliance under nominal and failover scenarios with dual-channel strategy.
Computational overhead analysis for continuous p95 aggregation within Teleoperation IM; validation that monitoring does not degrade control responsiveness.
